title: Ridgeline Rovers FC crest: clean up a phone-shot patch photo, vectorize the emblem, and build a factory-ready embroidery/patch vector package
grounding_note: VERBATIM from the Freelancer.com brief ("Realistic Textile Embroidery Design"): "I'm looking for a skilled embroidery designer to create a realistic logo. Requirements: - Realism style - Medium size (3-6 inches) - Experience with logo designs and embroidery techniques ... - Proficiency in embroidery digitization - Strong understanding of realistic design elements - Attention to detail and quality craftsmanship." The related Freelancer.com candidate "Premium Heritage Apparel Identity System & Factory-Ready Vector Engineering" supplies the production framing VERBATIM: "Translate existing conceptual ... design mockups into a finalized, technically precise, and production-ready 2D vector asset system ... meticulously engineered to accommodate physical apparel manufacturing techniques, specifically high-density direct embroidery, woven patches with merrowed borders." REASONABLY ENRICHED to make it a runnable Adobe-connector benchmark: the original brief implies embroidery DIGITIZING (producing stitch files such as .dst/.emb/.pes), which is NOT in Adobe connector scope — no connector emits stitch files. The genuinely-doable, embroidery-adjacent work the printer/embroiderer actually rewards is the FRONT HALF of that pipeline: taking the club's only reference (a flat phone photo of a sample patch stitched off an earlier draft), cleaning it up (deskew, tone, neutralize fabric cast, crush the twill weave to a clean high-contrast silhouette, knock the ground transparent), VECTORIZING it into razor-sharp scalable art at the requested 3-6 inch patch size, then reducing it to the flat 4-thread-color spot separations an embroidery/patch factory needs and packaging clean SVG plus exported vector masters. The stitch-file digitizing itself is honestly declared out of connector scope; no unrelated deliverable is invented. Fictional IP-clean brand "Ridgeline Rovers FC" replaces any real club/company.
one_line_ask: Clean up the club's phone photo of a stitched sample crest, vectorize the emblem into razor-sharp scalable art at 3-6 inch patch size, reduce it to flat spot-color separations, and hand back a factory-ready SVG plus exported vector masters.
full_brief:
Ridgeline Rovers FC, an amateur mountain-town football (soccer) club, is ordering embroidered crest patches and sleeve badges from a factory and needs its emblem turned into proper production-ready vector artwork first. The only artwork the club has is a phone photo of a sample patch that a previous designer stitched off an early draft (crest_sample_patch.jpg) — a shield-shaped crest with a stylized mountain-ridge silhouette, a football, the letters "RRFC", and the founding year "EST. 2014" on a banner ribbon, stitched in four thread colors (forest green, cream, slate grey, black outline) on a heathered fabric backing. The photo is a real hand-held shot: tilted about 3 degrees, dull and slightly underexposed, a cool/green color cast from the fabric, and the twill weave texture shows across every color so the edges look fuzzy. That texture and cast make it useless for a factory as-is.

The job: (1) clean the reference so it can be traced — deskew it level, auto-tone to fix the flat exposure, neutralize the green fabric cast so the four thread colors read true, then push contrast hard to crush the woven-fabric texture and drive every element toward a clean, flat, high-contrast silhouette; knock the heathered backing to transparent so the trace has no halo. (2) VECTORIZE the cleaned crest into a clean, scalable SVG — this is the master emblem, and it must stay razor-sharp when the factory scales it to the requested medium 3-6 inch patch size. (3) Because embroidery and woven patches need each color as one flat solid thread, reduce the emblem to FLAT SPOT SEPARATIONS: go element by element, select each color region, and lock it to a single solid ink from the club's 4-color palette (no gradients, no anti-aliased blends), then pull a single-color BLACK OUTLINE plate the factory uses for the merrowed-border stitch path, and re-vectorize the flattened art. (4) Deliver a one-color KNOCKOUT (solid emblem silhouette) the factory can use to cut the twill/felt backing. (5) Export the artwork to vector masters (PDF/PNG/SVG) from a genuine authored .ai so the club and factory have the emblem in every format the press needs before the run.

Palette: forest green #2E5A3A, cream #F2E9D0, slate grey #6B7078, black #111111 outline. Deliverables: master crest SVG, flat spot-separated SVG, black-outline plate, one-color knockout, and exported vector masters. Stitch digitizing (.dst/.pes) is handled by the factory from these vector masters — it is not part of this hand-off.

inputs.name: crest_sample_patch.jpg
inputs.gen_prompt: A real hand-held phone photo of a single embroidered football-club crest patch lying flat on a wooden table. The patch is a shield/heraldic shield shape with a stylized mountain-ridge silhouette across the top, a small football (soccer ball) in the center, the bold uppercase letters 'RRFC' beneath it, and a banner ribbon at the bottom reading 'EST. 2014'. It is stitched in exactly four thread colors: forest green, cream, slate grey, and a black outline, on a heathered grey-green fabric backing, with a slightly raised satin-stitch merrowed border. The photo is slightly tilted about 3 degrees clockwise, dull and mildly underexposed with soft indoor lighting, a faint cool green color cast, and the woven twill thread texture is clearly visible across every color so the edges look soft and fuzzy rather than crisp. Documentary, true-to-life phone-camera realism, real thread sheen and fabric weave, no CGI gloss, no studio lighting. 2400x2400.
inputs.realism_notes: Must read as an actual phone photo of a real stitched sample: visible satin/twill thread texture across every fill, a raised merrowed border, mild ~3 degree keystone tilt, dull underexposure and a green fabric cast — so auto_straighten / auto_tone / temperature-neutralize / contrast-crush all have genuine work to do before a clean trace is possible. The four thread colors must be distinct and the RRFC / EST. 2014 lettering legible so select_by_prompt can target each color region and the vectorize yields clean glyph and shield paths. gemini-3-pro-image chosen for legible embedded lettering.

inputs.name: ridgeline_crest.ai
inputs.gen_prompt: AUTHORED DELIVERABLE (cannot be model-generated): a genuine Adobe Illustrator document (.ai) whose vector geometry is placed/assembled by hand from the traced ridgeline_crest_master.svg and ridgeline_crest_spot_separated.svg produced earlier in the chain — the master emblem and its 4-flat-ink spot-separated version arranged on artboards sized for a 3-6 inch patch, with the four club spot inks defined as named swatches (#2E5A3A / #F2E9D0 / #6B7078 / #111111). No new art is drawn; it only hosts the traced paths so document_render_vector can EXPORT vector masters from it.
inputs.realism_notes: Modeled as the freelancer's own Illustrator working file built ON TOP of the connector-traced SVGs (a normal hand-off). document_render_vector is [C] but only EXPORTS a genuine authored .ai; it never composes the emblem, so the .ai is listed as a supplied/authored input, its geometry derived entirely from the earlier image_vectorize outputs.

outputs.name: ridgeline_crest_master.svg
outputs.spec: Clean vectorized master crest (full shield: mountain ridge + football + RRFC + EST. 2014 banner) as a scalable SVG with crisp continuous paths and transparent ground, razor-sharp at the requested 3-6 inch patch size; the master from which the .ai/PDF/PNG exports derive. Four-color club palette preserved (#2E5A3A / #F2E9D0 / #6B7078 / #111111).

outputs.name: ridgeline_crest_spot_separated.svg
outputs.spec: Flat spot-separated SVG: every element reduced to a single solid ink from the 4-color palette (no gradients, no anti-aliased blends) so each color prints/stitches as one flat thread — the factory-ready separation version.

outputs.name: ridgeline_crest_black_outline_plate.png
outputs.spec: Single-color black outline plate (solid black emblem linework on transparent) giving the factory the merrowed-border and detail-stitch path; hi-res PNG.

outputs.name: ridgeline_crest_knockout.png
outputs.spec: One-color solid knockout of the whole shield silhouette (flat fill on transparent) the factory uses to cut the twill/felt patch backing to shape.

outputs.name: ridgeline_crest_vector_masters
outputs.spec: Exported vector masters (PDF + PNG + SVG) rendered from the genuine authored .ai built off the traced SVGs — for the factory press and for club use.

connector_workflow.note: Open an upload session to bring the club's phone photo of the stitched sample patch into Adobe CC storage.
Finalize the upload to a stable presigned asset URI that the image_* tools can consume as the working reference.
Correct the ~3 degree hand-held tilt so the shield sits level and the banner baseline is true before tracing — straight geometry traces cleaner.
Auto-tone the dull, mildly underexposed photo so the four thread colors gain clean separation in value.
Neutralize the cool/green fabric color cast so forest green, cream, slate grey and black each read true rather than tinted by the backing.

Push contrast hard to crush the woven twill/satin texture across every color and drive each element toward a flat high-contrast silhouette — critical so vectorize traces emblem edges, not thread weave.
Knock the heathered fabric backing to true transparent so the trace has no halo/fringe around the shield edge (->transparent PNG only, no reconstruction).
CORE DELIVERABLE 1: raster->clean SVG trace of the cleaned crest = MASTER emblem, razor-sharp for the 3-6 inch patch scale; basis for the .ai/PDF/PNG exports.
SPOT-SEPARATION PASS: select the forest-green mountain-ridge region so it can be locked to one flat ink.
Solid-fill the selected ridge region with the exact palette green #2E5A3A (SOLID color only) so it becomes one flat embroidery thread with no gradient or anti-aliased blend.
Select the cream football/panel region on the partially-flattened art to lock the next thread color.
Solid-fill the cream region with #F2E9D0 (SOLID color only) — second flat spot ink locked.
Select the slate-grey RRFC lettering / banner fill so it too reduces to a single flat ink.
Solid-fill the slate region with #6B7078 (SOLID color only) — third flat spot ink locked; the black outline remains, giving a clean 4-flat-ink plate ready to re-vectorize.
CORE DELIVERABLE 2: re-vectorize the flattened 4-flat-ink plate -> factory-ready spot-separated SVG where each color is one solid thread.
Select just the black outline / detail linework of the emblem to build the border-stitch plate.
Invert so everything except the black linework is targeted, ready to clear it away and leave only the outline plate.
Solid-fill the inverted (non-outline) area to clear/transparent leaving the solid black outline plate (SOLID fill only) -> the merrowed-border / detail-stitch path deliverable.
Apply a solid black color overlay to the whole cleaned shield silhouette -> one-color KNOCKOUT the factory uses to cut the twill/felt patch backing to the shield shape (in-scope solid recolor).

Open an upload session to bring in the genuine authored .ai vector master (built off the traced ridgeline_crest_master.svg / spot-separated SVG in Illustrator) so it can be exported — render_vector only EXPORTS a real .ai, it never composes art.
Finalize the authored .ai upload to a stable URI for export; the vector geometry derives entirely from the traced SVGs above (no from-scratch drawing).
Export the genuine authored .ai to the vector masters (PDF + PNG + SVG) for the factory press and club use. document_render_vector only EXPORTS the supplied .ai; it does not compose the emblem.

chaining_note: Single connected chain, no orphan steps. The club's raw phone photo is ingested (steps 1-2) into a stable asset URI, which feeds a linear cleanup pipeline: auto_straighten(3) -> auto_tone(4) -> color_temperature neutralize(5) -> brightness_contrast crush(6) -> remove_background(7), yielding crest_clean_transparent — the hub asset. That hub is vectorized into the MASTER SVG (8). The spot-separation sub-chain runs strictly output->input off the hub: select green(9) -> fill green(10, consuming hub + mask) = crest_green_flat -> select cream(11) -> fill cream(12) = crest_two_flat -> select slate(13) -> fill slate(14) = crest_spot_flat_raster, which is re-vectorized into the SPOT-SEPARATED SVG (15). The same flat raster feeds the outline plate sub-chain: select outline(16) -> invert(17) -> fill_area on the inverted ground(18) = black-outline plate. The cleaned hub is also color-overlaid to the one-color knockout (19). The authored .ai (built off the two traced SVGs) is uploaded (20->21 authored_ai_uri) and document_render_vector EXPORTS it to the vector masters (22). Every step after ingest reads a prior step's named output; the factory-ready vector emblem emerges only from image_vectorize, never from an authored-from-scratch draw.
difficulty_rationale: Long-horizon and wide-mix: 22 connector calls, 13 distinct Adobe tools across three groups (asset ingest/export, the image_* raster/vector family, and vector-master export). Difficulty comes from correct ordering (deskew + tone + cast-neutralize + contrast-crush + background-knock all BEFORE vectorize, so the trace follows emblem edges and not the woven twill texture), and from a genuinely non-trivial spot-separation sub-chain — three sequential select_by_prompt -> solid fill_area passes that lock each of three thread colors to one flat ink, then a re-vectorize, plus an invert-based black-outline-plate extraction. It stays strictly inside connector limits: no generative fill, no AI object removal / inpainting, no text-to-image or from-scratch letterform design, no upscaling, no compositing, no layout composition; image_fill_area is used strictly as solid-color flattening (the ONLY honest non-generative way to enforce flat spot inks for embroidery); the vector deliverables come from image_vectorize, and the exported masters come from document_render_vector EXPORTING a genuine authored .ai supplied as an uploaded input, not composed by the connector. The embroidery stitch-file digitizing the raw brief implies is honestly declared out of scope and left to the factory. All Adobe steps are [C] headless-confirmed, so the task is fully autonomous-doable while remaining a demanding, faithful real-client brief.

reverify.banned_capability_check: Confirmed NONE of the banned capabilities are used. Emblem geometry is TRACED by image_vectorize (n8, n15) from a cleaned real photo — no text-to-image / from-scratch art/logo/letterform. image_remove_background (n7) only knocks the heathered backing transparent — no inpainting/object-removal-with-reconstruction. image_fill_area (n10/n12/n14/n18) is SOLID-COLOR fill only (flat spot-ink flattening + clearing the outline-plate ground) — no generative expand/outpaint, no canvas growth. No upscaling/super-resolution. document_render_vector (n22) only EXPORTS a genuine authored .ai supplied as an uploaded input (n20->n21, kind:data, gen_model:'n/a-authored-asset'), never composes the emblem. All actions are real Adobe connector tools (22 [C] headless). Embroidery stitch-file digitizing (.dst/.pes) is explicitly declared out of connector scope and handed to the factory.
reverify.notes: Trimmed two low-value extras to reduce expert effort: the standalone thread-color/typography reference note (local_write_recipe_note + its feeding font_recommend step) and the shared CC review folder (asset_create_folders + asset_copy_assets + asset_inline_preview) that merely re-bundled already-listed deliverables with a preview. Five core production deliverables remain — master crest SVG, spot-separated SVG, black-outline plate, one-color knockout, and exported vector masters. Renumbered to a 22-step chain; tool_call_count=22, distinct_adobe_tools=13; chain remains valid, connected, gen-free, and fully Adobe-connector-doable.
